Experimental Evidence for CRM1-mediated: Localization assays (using GFP2 fusions in wild-type, crm1 temperature-sensitive, and LMB-sensitive crm1 mutant strains), mutagenesis, leptomycin B sensitivity.
Functional Export Signals: Residues 96-102 (core NES), and residues 87-114 (region tested as a fusion).
Mutations That Affect CRM1 Binding: Mutations in the Nap1p NES (residues 96–102), specifically the `nap1-1` mutant where four leucines or isoleucines were changed to alanines, resulted in clearly nuclear localization, indicating impaired Crm1p-dependent export.
Mutations That Affect Nuclear Export: L96A, L98A, I100A, L102A (within residues 96-102 of Nap1p, referred to as nap1-1 mutant).
Other Residues Important for Export: The region spanning residues 87–114 of Nap1p, which includes the core leucine-rich NES (residues 96–102), was found to be actively exported when fused to GFP2. This suggests that residues flanking the 96-102 core also contribute to the export function.
is_NesDB_doubt: While a Crm1p-dependent leucine-rich NES (residues 96-102) was identified and validated, the export of full-length Nap1p showed only negligible or very small defects in crm1 mutant strains and with LMB treatment, suggesting the existence of a second, Crm1p-independent export pathway for Nap1p.

Experimental Evidence for CRM1-mediated: Leptomycin B sensitivity, localization assays (using GFP fusion proteins, microinjection), mutagenesis
Full Name: Stat1 (Signal transducer and activator of transcription 1)
Mutations That Affect CRM1 Binding: The double-mutant Leu308,312Ala of Stat1 showed markedly different export kinetics from wild-type Stat1, with most of the protein still sequestered in the nucleus after 3 hours. Even after 10 hours, only a considerable fraction was detectable outside the nucleus. Furthermore, the addition of the export inhibitor LMB did not further decrease the export rate of this Stat1 mutant. The text states, "Additionally, the mutant no longer displays sensitivity to the effects of the export inhibitor LMB (compare Figs. 1 and 5). ... As to the nature of this activity, LMB insensitivity indicates the existence of additional export mechanisms that do not rely on the export receptor CRM1."
Mutations That Affect Nuclear Export: Leu308Ala, Leu312Ala (double mutation)
Other Residues Important for Export: Further truncation by five residues at the N terminus of helix 4C abolished the activity of the peptide.
Secondary Structure of Export Signal: Helix (specifically, helix 4 of the coiled-coil domain)
is_NesDB_doubt: The modest nuclear export activity of Stat3 404–414 in the Rev1.4 assay contradicted published observations on this element in Stat1. Moreover, it has been suggested recently that this element may function as an NLS, as well, potentially confusing evaluation of this sequence in isolation, i.e., fused to a GFP-tagged reporter protein such as Rev1.4.

Experimental Evidence for CRM1-mediated: NEX-TRAP assay, mutational analysis, NES matching PKI-like consensus sequence, structural modeling with CRM1.
Mutations That Affect CRM1 Binding: Mutations were introduced into the nuclear export sequences (NESs) of pUL4, pUL13, pUL21, pUL37d11, pUL47, and pUL48 by replacing the three last consensus residues (Φ) with alanines (e.g., Lx2-3Lx2LxL to Lx2-3Ax2AxA). These NES mutations caused the proteins to remain in the nucleus, preventing their recruitment to the TGN for FRB-FKBP-complex formation, thereby abolishing nuclear export. Specifically for pUL48/VP16, the wild-type NES TARLARVLFLHLYL was replaced by the mutant sequence TARLARVLFAHAYL, which resulted in single cells positive for ICP0 but no plaques, indicating a defect in viral replication due to impaired nuclear export.
Other Residues Important for Export: The NES of pUL13 is characterized by two prolines at the N-terminal end of LPPELKPLLVL, reminiscent of a Rev NES and matching the consensus Φ0Φ1xΦ2(x)2Φ3xΦ4 with Φ1 = P >> L.
Secondary Structure of Export Signal: mostly α-helical
is_NesDB_doubt: Bioinformatic prediction of nuclear export activity appears to be insufficient and there is still an essential need for experimental validation of predicted NESs by a suitable assay such as the NEX-TRAP assay.

Mutations That Affect Nuclear Export: Alanine substitutions at the leucine residues of the Mcm3 leucine-rich motif (AQR-RAQAGA) delay nuclear export. Alanine substitutions in the five full CDK consensus sites (mcm3-cdk5A) or four sites flanking the Mcm3 NLS (mcm3-cdk4A) result in constitutive nuclear localization of a GFP3 fusion protein and partial nuclear retention of GFP-Mcm3. A combined mutation of four CDK consensus sites and the leucine-rich motif of the NES (mcm3-cdk4A-nes) completely abrogates net nuclear export.
Other Residues Important for Export: CDK consensus phosphorylation sites on Mcm3, located within the NLS-NES transport module. Phosphorylation of these sites promotes net nuclear export.
is_NesDB_doubt: the NES is not absolutely required for the net nuclear export of Mcm3.

Experimental Evidence for CRM1-mediated: Leptomycin B sensitivity, localization assays (confocal microscopy, cryoimmunogold electron microscopy), mutagenesis, and surface plasmon resonance (SPR) spectroscopy binding assays.
Functional Export Signals: The Nuclear Export Signal (NES) is located within amino acids 35-55 of the VEEV capsid protein (part of the H68 peptide). This region includes a conserved helix I (amino acids 35-47) and critical hydrophobic residues Leu-48 and Phe-50. The CRM1 binding site extends up to Asp-55, with the sequence K49QRRD (amino acids 49-53) specifically involved in strong CRM1 binding. The core element of the supraNES is described as a 9-amino-acid-long leucine-rich alpha peptide, with an additional 5 amino acids downstream also critical for its function.
Mutations That Affect CRM1 Binding: Replacement of Leu-48 and Phe-50 with alanines (H68AA1-GFP) abolished detectable binding to CRM1. The sequence K49QRRD (amino acids 49-53) was involved in strong binding of the H68-GFP fusion to CRM1.
Mutations That Affect Nuclear Export: Replacement of Leu-48 and Phe-50 with alanines (H68AA1-GFP) completely abolished the inhibition of nuclear import and led to accumulation of the protein in the nuclei. A point mutation H68E51-GFP in the supraNES also downregulated nuclear import and led to nuclear accumulation. Deletions in helix I (amino acids 35 to 47) led to accumulation of the mutant capsid protein in nuclei. Deletion H68del54-56-GFP became mostly nuclear. Replacement of two arginines downstream of the NES with alanines (H68AA3-GFP) reduced the ability to inhibit nuclear import.
Other Residues Important for Export: An additional 5 amino acids located downstream of the 9-amino-acid core are critical for the functioning of the supraNES. The sequence K49QRRD (amino acids 49-53) is involved in strong CRM1 binding. The specific spacing and sequence of the linker peptide between the NES and NLS domains are also important for the ability of the H68 peptide to inhibit nuclear import.
Secondary Structure of Export Signal: Alpha peptide (specifically, a 9-amino-acid-long leucine-rich alpha peptide core), which is part of a conserved helix I.

Experimental Evidence for CRM1-mediated: Leptomycin B sensitivity, localization assays (measuring nuclear accumulation), mutagenesis
Mutations That Affect CRM1 Binding: Substitution of LI104/5 to AG (NESm) in the CRM1-recognized nuclear export sequence (NES) significantly increased nuclear accumulation in SR40 cells, implying impaired export. Additionally, the E108-substituted derivative of VP3, which approximates T108-prephosphorylated VP3, accumulated in the nucleus of SR40 cells to a level comparable to the wild-type protein in SAOS-2 cells, with no increase in nuclear localization effected by leptomycin B. This is consistent with the idea that phosphorylation of VP3 at T108 (adjacent to the NES) inhibits NES action, which is CRM1-recognized.
Mutations That Affect Nuclear Export: LI104/5 to AG (NESm), T108 to E
Other Residues Important for Export: Threonine 108 (T108)

Mutations That Affect Nuclear Export: L1168G mutation in NES-like 4 of NLS-mDia2-(411-1171) caused nuclear accumulation, indicating impaired export. However, the same L1168G mutation in full-length mDia2 did not abolish CRM1-dependent export, suggesting other export mechanisms.
Organism: Mouse (mouse mDia2, NIH3T3 cells) and Human (HeLa cells)
Other Residues Important for Export: The N-terminal region (1-411) is suggested to contain an unrecognized NES or bind to another CRM1-exported protein, as mutations in NES-like 3 within this region did not abolish export, and mutation in NES-like 4 in full-length mDia2 did not abolish export.
is_NesDB_doubt: These findings suggest that the NES-like 3 does not function as a NES in intact protein.

Alternative Names: Armadillo protein p120ctn, p120ctn isoforms, isoform 3AB, isoform 3A, isoform 3AC, isoform 3B, isoform 3N
Experimental Evidence for CRM1-mediated: leptomycin B sensitivity, localization assays, mutagenesis, microinjection assays
Mutations That Affect Nuclear Export: leucine-844 to alanine, leucine-846 to alanine

Experimental Evidence for CRM1-mediated: CRM-1 dependent pathway; Leptomycin B (LMB) sensitivity; Immunofluorescence staining and confocal microscopy analysis showing colocalization of p28GANK and RelA in the nucleus in the presence of LMB, and cytoplasmic sequestration of RelA by p28GANK.
Functional Export Signals: N-terminal tail domain (amino acids 1-38) of p28GANK.
Mutations That Affect Nuclear Export: Deletion of the N-terminal tail domain (amino acids 1-38) of p28GANK resulted in nuclear localization of the mutant protein and failure to export RelA to the cytoplasm.
Other Residues Important for Export: All five ankyrin repeats and the C-terminal tail domain (amino acids 39-226) of p28GANK are required for its binding to RelA. This association is crucial for p28GANK to accelerate the nuclear export of RelA, suggesting these residues, beyond the N-terminal NES, influence the export of the NF-κB:p28GANK complex.
is_NesDB_doubt: true

Mutations That Affect Nuclear Export: T48 in CA04-NEP

Experimental Evidence for CRM1-mediated: Leptomycin B sensitivity, localization assays (GFP-tagged constructs, C-terminal deletions), site-directed mutagenesis, nuclear import assay in yeast.
